questions.question: Which empire built the Colosseum in Rome?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum was built between 70–80 AD under Emperors Vespasian and Titus of the Roman Empire. It could hold an estimated 50,000–80,000 spectators and hosted gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, and public spectacles.
questions.memory_hook: Rome wasn't built in a day — but the Colosseum was built for days of entertainment.

questions.question: What was the name of the first artificial satellite launched into orbit?
questions.explanation: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957, becoming the first artificial satellite to orbit Earth. Its launch shocked the United States and triggered the Space Race. It orbited for three weeks before its batteries failed.
questions.memory_hook: Sputnik — sounds like something that goes 'beep beep' in space.

questions.question: The 1930 FIFA World Cup was the first ever held. Which country won, and why was the tournament historically unique?
options: Brazil won; it was the only World Cup played without European nations due to WWII tensions
Uruguay won on home soil; European nations had to travel by ship, and many refused — making it the only World Cup with significant no-shows from major footballing nations
Argentina won; the final was replayed after a disputed refereeing decision
Italy won; the tournament was held across three continents simultaneously for the first time
questions.answer: Uruguay won on home soil; European nations had to travel by ship, and many refused — making it the only World Cup with significant no-shows from major footballing nations
questions.explanation: Uruguay hosted and won the inaugural World Cup, beating Argentina 4-2 in the final in Montevideo. The two-week Atlantic crossing deterred most European teams — only France, Belgium, Romania, and Yugoslavia made the trip. The US reached the semi-finals. Uruguay also won the 1928 Olympics football tournament, making them the dominant footballing nation of the era.
questions.memory_hook: The 1930 World Cup was Uruguay's home show — most European nations skipped the two-week Atlantic crossing, so Uruguay beat Argentina 4-2 in the final to claim the first-ever world title.

questions.question: What is the diameter of a standard basketball hoop in inches?
questions.explanation: A standard NBA basketball hoop has an inner diameter of 18 inches (45.7 cm). A standard basketball has a diameter of about 9.4 inches, meaning two balls could theoretically fit through the hoop at once.
questions.memory_hook: 18 inches — think of an 18-year-old going pro.

questions.question: Which artist sang 'Billie Jean' and 'Thriller'?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson released 'Billie Jean' and 'Thriller' on the 1982 album 'Thriller,' which remains the best-selling album of all time with over 70 million copies sold. The 'Thriller' music video, directed by John Landis, is considered one of the greatest ever made.
questions.memory_hook: Jackson 5 became Jackson 1 — and then 'Thriller' made him Jackson of the world.

questions.question: Which film won the first Academy Award for Best Picture?
options: All Quiet on the Western Front
questions.explanation: Wings (1927) won the first Academy Award for Best Picture at the inaugural ceremony in 1929. The award ceremony covered films released between 1927 and 1928. Wings was a World War I aerial combat film.
questions.memory_hook: Wings — the first Best Picture winner took flight.

questions.question: Who wrote the music for the film 'Star Wars'?
questions.explanation: John Williams composed the iconic Star Wars score, which premiered with the original film in 1977. The main theme is one of the most recognizable pieces of music in history. Williams has composed scores for Jaws, Indiana Jones, Schindler's List, and many others.
questions.memory_hook: John Williams — when you hear those brass notes, you know.

questions.question: Which war was ended by the Treaty of Versailles?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles, signed on June 28, 1919, officially ended World War I between Germany and the Allied Powers. Its harsh terms — including reparations and territorial losses — contributed to conditions that led to World War II.
questions.memory_hook: Versailles ended WWI — and planted seeds for WWII.

questions.question: Which strait separates Europe from Africa at its narrowest point?
questions.explanation: The Strait of Gibraltar is only about 14 kilometers wide at its narrowest point, connecting the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea between Spain and Morocco. It has been a strategically critical waterway for thousands of years and was considered the edge of the known world in ancient times.
questions.memory_hook: Gibraltar = the door between continents. 14 km wide, two continents, two oceans. Ancient sailors called it the Pillars of Hercules — the end of the world.

questions.question: Which planet in our solar system currently has the most confirmed moons?
questions.explanation: Saturn leads with 146 confirmed moons as of 2023, edging past Jupiter's 95. Many of Saturn's moons are small and were only discovered recently using advanced telescopes. Its largest moon, Titan, is bigger than the planet Mercury and has a thick atmosphere and liquid methane lakes.
questions.memory_hook: Saturn won the moon count recently. 146 moons and counting. Titan alone is bigger than Mercury — Saturn's biggest moon is a planet-sized world in its own right.

questions.question: The Strait of Hormuz — through which about 20% of the world's oil passes — connects the Persian Gulf to which body of water?
questions.explanation: The Strait of Hormuz connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and is the world's most important oil chokepoint. At its narrowest, it is only 33 km wide. Iran borders the strait to the north and Oman to the south. Roughly 20 million barrels of oil pass through it daily. Any closure would cause an immediate global energy crisis and has been a recurring geopolitical pressure point.
questions.memory_hook: Strait of Hormuz = Persian Gulf → Gulf of Oman. 33 km wide at the narrowest. 20 million barrels of oil daily. Iran on one side, Oman on the other. The most strategically critical waterway in the world for energy.

questions.question: In 2006, the International Astronomical Union voted to reclassify Pluto from a planet to a 'dwarf planet.' What three criteria must a body meet to be classified as a planet?
options: A planet must orbit the Sun, be large enough that its gravity has made it spherical, and have cleared the neighborhood around its orbit of other debris — Pluto fails the third criterion
A planet must orbit the Sun, have a diameter greater than 2,000 km, and possess at least one natural moon — Pluto is slightly too small at 2,377 km
A planet must orbit the Sun, have a solid or gaseous surface, and exert gravitational influence on at least one other planetary body — Pluto technically qualifies by this definition
A planet must orbit the Sun, not be a moon of another body, and have an internal heat source — Pluto has no measurable internal heat after its core cooled
questions.answer: A planet must orbit the Sun, be large enough that its gravity has made it spherical, and have cleared the neighborhood around its orbit of other debris — Pluto fails the third criterion
questions.explanation: Pluto sits in the Kuiper Belt — a region of icy bodies beyond Neptune — and shares its orbital neighborhood with thousands of similar objects. It hasn't gravitationally dominated its zone, as Earth and Jupiter have. The 2006 vote was controversial and close (424 votes to 237) because many astronomers weren't present; Pluto's discoverer Clyde Tombaugh's ashes actually flew past Pluto on New Horizons in 2015. New Horizons revealed a heart-shaped nitrogen-ice plain (Tombaugh Regio) and mountains of water ice on Pluto's surface.
questions.memory_hook: Pluto was downgraded because it hasn't cleared its orbital neighborhood — it shares the Kuiper Belt with thousands of similar icy bodies. The controversial 2006 vote was 424-237; Clyde Tombaugh's ashes flew past Pluto on New Horizons in 2015.
